Welcome back to The Best and the Brightest. In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s edition, my interview with Rep. Elissa Slotkin, the Democratic senator-elect who won where the top of her ticket lost: Michigan. As a former C.I.A. analyst and Pentagon official, Slotkin will likely be a key Democratic voice in the coming confirmation battles of both Tulsi Gabbard and Pete Hegseth. And she had some very frank opinions to share about Gabbard. \xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x8c

Welcome back to The Best and the Brightest, I\xe2\x80\x99m Abby Livingston .

In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s edition, my interview with Rep. Elissa Slotkin , the Democratic senator-elect who won where the top of her ticket lost: Michigan. As a former C.I.A. analyst and Pentagon official, Slotkin will likely be a key Democratic voice in the coming confirmation battles of both Tulsi Gabbard and Pete Hegseth . And she had some very frank opinions to share about Gabbard.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6.

  • Dylan on Joe & Mika\xe2\x80\x99s Mar-a-Lago adventure : On Monday morning, MSNBC\xe2\x80\x99s connubial co-hosts Joe Scarborough and Mika Brzezinski informed their loyal audience of almost a million viewers that they had recently ventured south from their home in Jupiter to Mar-a-Lago. There, for the first time in seven years, they met with Donald Trump \xe2\x80\x94the very man they\xe2\x80\x99d spent the last several years vociferously pillorying. Reading from their respective teleprompters with well-choreographed solemnity, the couple conveyed this relatively benign news as though they were disclosing their involvement in a top-secret nuclear negotiation. Or, as CNN\xe2\x80\x99s John Berman cheekily put it over on the rival network, \xe2\x80\x9cas if it were the Yalta summit.\xe2\x80\x9d

    Anticipating some blowback from their mostly liberal coalition, Joe and Mika cast the Trump meeting as an attempt to foster a constructive dialogue. \xe2\x80\x9cFor those asking why we would go speak to the president-elect during such fraught times, especially between us, I guess I would ask back, \xe2\x80\x98Why wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t we?\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d Mika said. \xe2\x80\x9cJoe and I realized it\xe2\x80\x99s time to do something different, and that starts with not only talking about Donald Trump, but also talking with him.\xe2\x80\x9d Later, Mika also alluded to her father, Zbigniew Brzezinski , a top U.S. diplomat who served as Jimmy Carter \xe2\x80\x99s national security advisor. She explained that maintaining an open dialogue was \xe2\x80\x9ca task shared by reporters and commentators alike.\xe2\x80\x9d

    In light of Joe and Mika\xe2\x80\x99s relatively exalted status in the legacy media firmament, the disclosure became a minor source of fascination or frustration among their peers at 30 Rock, as well as fodder for the media\xe2\x80\x99s broader introspective post-election soul-searching journey. At MSNBC, weekend host Katie Phang wrote that \xe2\x80\x9cnormalizing Trump is a bad idea. Period,\xe2\x80\x9d while Stephanie Ruhle sought to convince her viewers that engagement was sound journalistic practice, and by no means a capitulation. Joe and Mika\xe2\x80\x99s pilgrimage earned write-ups from the Times , the Journal , CNN, and so forth. It also induced predictable hysterics from the chronically outraged gasbag class, including Keith Olbermann and Megyn Kelly . Meanwhile, the Morning Joe audience, already at a post-election nadir, dropped 17 percent in the hour after Joe and Mika revealed their Trump meeting. The following day, the show\xe2\x80\x99s ratings were down 38 percent from this year\xe2\x80\x99s average.

    Alas, the debate over statecraft vs. surrender diverted attention from the real palace intrigue. After all, Joe and Mika\xe2\x80\x99s paeans to diplomacy belied the true motivation for their visit to Mar-a-Lago. In fact, the couple made the trek to Palm Beach because they feared retribution, a fact that has thus far only been alluded to by CNN\xe2\x80\x99s Brian Stelter . This week, sources with direct knowledge of Joe and Mika\xe2\x80\x99s thinking provided me with more details on those fears that, in addition to underscoring the batshit-crazy nature of this moment in American politics, also offer insight into the potential chilling effect that Trump\xe2\x80\x99s return to the Oval Office might have on the news media writ large, and especially MSNBC. \xe2\x80\x94 Dylan Byers [ Read More ]

A MESSAGE FROM INSTAGRAM
Instagram Teen Accounts: automatic protections for teens

Parents want safer online experiences for their teens. That's why Instagram is introducing Teen Accounts, with automatic protections for who can contact teens and the content they can see.

A key factor: Only parents can approve safety setting changes for teens under 16.

Learn More
  • Bill Cohan on the Treasury sweepstakes : Marc Rowan is almost certainly too normal to be Donald Trump \xe2\x80\x99s treasury secretary, especially in a cabinet composed mostly of quasi-lunatics without any qualifications for their jobs. Nevertheless, I\xe2\x80\x99m told that Marc is flying back from Hong Kong to meet Trump at Mar-a-Lago today, and that the position is his to lose. In fact, assuming the two men get along, an announcement could be imminent. As everyone on Wall Street knows, you don\xe2\x80\x99t get many chances to be treasury secretary. If chosen and confirmed, Rowan would be the wealthiest treasury secretary ever\xe2\x80\x94even wealthier than his new boss.

    Rowan, of course, already has a big-time job as the C.E.O. of Apollo Global Management, running a business with some 5,000 employees, around $700 billion of assets under management, and a market value of nearly $100 billion\xe2\x80\x94up nearly 250 percent since he took over from Leon Black in 2021. With all the funds that Apollo controls and that Rowan has a partnership stake in, he will have a difficult time unwinding himself from the company. John Paulson , another once-possible pick for treasury secretary, took himself out of contention after concluding that it would be too complicated to untangle himself from his hedge fund, or so we\xe2\x80\x99re told. I\xe2\x80\x99ve got to believe Marc would have an even harder time.

    But I can\xe2\x80\x99t think of a smarter, more qualified candidate, and he\xe2\x80\x99ll probably take the job if Trump offers it. Black, who remains Apollo\xe2\x80\x99s largest individual shareholder by far, agrees. He told me in a statement: \xe2\x80\x9cPresident Trump is a great businessman and the most financially savvy president of my lifetime. As with his first term, I\xe2\x80\x99m confident he will successfully pick someone who is very talented and smart. I\xe2\x80\x99ve known Marc Rowan for more than 40 years, and he is exceptionally bright, strategic, capable, loyal, and articulate, which is why I chose him as my successor. He would make an outstanding treasury secretary, if he is chosen.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94 Bill Cohan [ Read More ]
About Tulsi Gabbard\xe2\x80\xa6
Senator-elect Elissa Slotkin worked for the C.I.A., the Pentagon, and the Department of Defense. And she\xe2\x80\x99s seen enough to have some legitimate fears about the woman Trump wants to oversee U.S. intelligence.
ABBY LIVINGSTON
There are few politicians more qualified to weigh in on Donald Trump \xe2\x80\x99s cage-rattling nominations of Tulsi Gabbard and Pete Hegseth than Rep. Elissa Slotkin , the Democratic senator-elect from Michigan\xe2\x80\x94a blue wall state, notably, that Kamala Harris lost. A former C.I.A. analyst and Department of Defense official, Slotkin has endured the confirmation process, herself, and offered an extremely candid opinion on the \xe2\x80\x9ccharacter and competence\xe2\x80\x9d required to run those agencies\xe2\x80\x94and the potentially life-or-death consequences if the Senate gets it wrong. In this lightly edited conversation, Slotkin discusses her fears of a politicized military, how Trump might bully Republicans into abolishing the filibuster, and why Gabbard could be a threat to the very intelligence apparatus she\xe2\x80\x99s seeking to lead. This conversation has been lightly edited and condensed for clarity.
A MESSAGE FROM INSTAGRAM
Introducing Instagram Teen Accounts: limits for teens, peace of mind for parents

Parents want their teens to grow and thrive - and to make sure they\xe2\x80\x99re staying safe.

That\xe2\x80\x99s why Instagram is launching Teen Accounts, with automatic protections limiting who can contact teens and the content they can see. Putting built-in limits in place for teens, so parents can have more peace of mind.

Learn More
The Situation Room
Abby Livingston : You were an acting assistant secretary at the Pentagon. Can you weigh in on the Pete Hegseth nomination ?

Elissa Slotkin : Look, as someone who will be sworn in this January, I take very seriously the constitutional role to provide advice and consent on nominations, and I\xe2\x80\x99m going to take each nomination individually and take them each very seriously. But I\xe2\x80\x99m also someone who has served in places like the Pentagon, and at the C.I.A. I was like employee number five when we created the Director of National Intelligence under George W. Bush . I\xe2\x80\x99ve seen these jobs up close, and they require people who are of character and competence, and that\xe2\x80\x99s going to be my standard for looking at all of these nominations, and that includes having a thorough background check.

We\xe2\x80\x99re looking for people who aren\xe2\x80\x99t going to use these positions for political retribution, but who are willing to speak truth to power, which is important in these roles. Because it\xe2\x80\x99s not just a parlor game, right? For Washington, who\xe2\x80\x99s going to get what job is like our version of Us magazine or whatever. But I know that if you put the wrong people in these national security roles, Americans can die here and abroad. If you get someone who\xe2\x80\x99s not of character or competence, and on top of that refuses to speak truth to the commander in chief, you\xe2\x80\x99re literally putting American lives at risk. And I won\xe2\x80\x99t support that. But I\xe2\x80\x99m going to have to wait to see what actually happens once I\xe2\x80\x99m sworn in, and I\xe2\x80\x99ll take each nomination on its own.

The Tulsi Gabbard nomination has sent shockwaves through the intelligence community. As an ex C.I.A. officer, what do you think? How are your contacts in the intelligence community reacting to it?

We can\xe2\x80\x99t have people walking into the Situation Room and telling the commander in chief what he wants to hear, particularly when it comes to our adversaries and things like strategic weapons and invasions of other countries. You just can\xe2\x80\x99t have that. There has to be something more important than political loyalty, and some of the decisions that Tulsi has made over the last five or eight years have confused the hell out of me. I\xe2\x80\x99m not saying America is perfect when we act abroad, but any day of the week, I prefer American leadership over Russian leadership or Chinese leadership. Remember, for the D.N.I., you\xe2\x80\x99re in charge of all 18 intelligence-community agencies. She has expressed views that seem to preference adversaries. Certainly, it gave me pause, when I heard the nomination.

The intelligence community, and folks at the Pentagon, are deeply hesitant about the future, and what kind of political litmus tests they\xe2\x80\x99re going to be expected to take in the early days of the Trump administration to prove their loyalty\xe2\x80\x94and therefore, what critical, classified information is actually going to get to the leaders of those agencies and the commander in chief.

I\xe2\x80\x99ve definitely heard from our allies, our English-speaking cousins, what is called the \xe2\x80\x9cFive Eyes\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94 us, the Canadians, the Brits, the Aussies, the New Zealanders. We share a tremendous amount of intelligence collection and analysis with our English-speaking cousins, and I certainly have heard concerns about what it means for them, their assets, their intelligence collection, what kind of things are going to be at risk if they disagree with someone who\xe2\x80\x99s in charge of the D.N.I. office or the C.I.A.

Do you think the Five Eyes can stay intact over the next four years?

Yes. It stayed intact in the first Trump administration, but I think everyone\xe2\x80\x99s really going to be holding their breath for the first three to six months.
Retribution & Resistance
You mentioned retribution. Are you worried about any kind of executive branch retribution directed at you personally?

All of us are wondering what the climate is going to be, because Trump has been open about retribution. He hasn\xe2\x80\x99t hidden that, and his choice of Matt Gaetz is an open shot across the bow that, I\xe2\x80\x99m going to put someone as the senior law enforcement official in my administration who has talked openly about political retribution . It\xe2\x80\x99s hard to miss.

So, certainly, I have thought about it. If I\xe2\x80\x99m going to be speaking out on an issue, and it bothers the Trump administration, what will they do in response? As a senator-elect, I\xe2\x80\x99m looking at hiring a Senate staff, and every senator typically has legal counsel on staff. And you better believe that\xe2\x80\x99s an important position to me right now.

You lived through Trump I\xe2\x80\x94that\xe2\x80\x99s how you came to office, in a lot of ways. What\xe2\x80\x99s your mindset going into Trump II?

We know that Trump is extremely good at throwing out red meat on five or 10 different issues in a given day. One of the mistakes Democrats made back in his first term was not separating the wheat from chaff. I thought a lot about that from a national security [perspective]. I gamed out a kind of decision tree on which issues I really must engage in, and which issues are more fleeting.

When Trump does something on a given day that I don\xe2\x80\x99t agree with, I think, is it tactical? Is it strategic? And then: Is it reversible or irreversible? And something that\xe2\x80\x99s irreversible and strategic should get my attention, and something that\xe2\x80\x99s tactical and reversible may not rise to the occasion. If we try to push back on everything, we\xe2\x80\x99ll be effective at nothing.

And obviously, as a national security person, what happens in the use of the active duty military is very important, and both strategic and irreversible. If we start deeply politicizing the military, using it as an arm of retribution, or contravening the Constitution by activating [the military] inside the United States, these are things that literally contravene the Constitution. I\xe2\x80\x99m trying to figure out how to be most effective against those threats.
You\xe2\x80\x99ll be in the minority as an incoming senator. What role can a senator play in pushing back against Trump?

I can\xe2\x80\x99t speak from experience, since I\xe2\x80\x99ve never been a senator, but it\xe2\x80\x99s better to be in the minority in the Senate than in the House, where you literally have no power. Because of the filibuster, there\xe2\x80\x99s still a role for senators on things like legislation and appropriations, but there\xe2\x80\x99s also the convening and messaging power of a senator. A senator can often corral all of those disparate groups into something more comprehensive and more strategic. And being one of 100 in a system where you don\xe2\x80\x99t have Democrats in the White House or in charge in the House means that you have a voice in the conversation just by showing up to work every day.

The filibuster was very out of fashion in the Democratic Party two years ago. Is there a feeling of relief among your future colleagues that it\xe2\x80\x99s still in place?

To be honest, what I\xe2\x80\x99m concerned about is that President Trump is going to come into the White House and be sworn in and start wanting all his initiatives to be passed, and he\xe2\x80\x99s going to put real pressure on Senator Thune and others to get rid of the filibuster. I think we\xe2\x80\x99re still in a to-be-determined position on the filibuster, because I can\xe2\x80\x99t imagine that President Trump will be satisfied if he can\xe2\x80\x99t get things passed with the narrow majority it looks like they\xe2\x80\x99ll have in the House. I think there are mixed feelings about what could happen to the filibuster.
The 2026 Map
You ran a campaign in Michigan and you won. The national ticket did not. What is the one thing you wish your fellow Democrats understood about this election?

If I had to boil it down to one thing, it\xe2\x80\x99s that our priorities need to reflect the priorities of the majority of citizens. That tends to be either their pocketbook or their kids. And we need to talk about those issues, not from the faculty lounge, but from the manufacturing floor.

Do you think Democrats are ready to listen to that message?

There\xe2\x80\x99s plenty of people saying it, but I come from the Pentagon, and when you have an operation that goes wrong, you do an after-action review, and then a lessons-learned process, and you change protocols for the next operation. Right now, there\xe2\x80\x99s certainly a number of people who accept that we need to be doing the after-action review. The question is, what do you do with that information, [and] once you\xe2\x80\x99ve come to consensus, how do you operationalize that? What are your lessons learned that you\xe2\x80\x99re going to change in the next cycle or the next year? It\xe2\x80\x99s that second part to me that\xe2\x80\x99s important, because otherwise we\xe2\x80\x99re just admiring the problem.

You and a handful of other Democratic senators and senators-elect managed to punch through and win, and Democrats did not do badly on the House side, either. One of my theories is maybe it all mitigated a wave. How did you see it from your vantage point?

Certainly, there was a wave going on across the country, and surviving it took real work. And it took reading the situation accurately, which means understanding that people\xe2\x80\x99s number one issue was the economy and inflation and the future of work. And if you weren\xe2\x80\x99t talking about those things, you were having half a conversation. And it\xe2\x80\x99s not that other issues aren\xe2\x80\x99t important. Of course I\xe2\x80\x99m concerned about democracy. Of course I\xe2\x80\x99m concerned about equality and rights and all those issues. But people had a hard time focusing on those issues if they couldn\xe2\x80\x99t make ends meet.

And I think those of us who survived internalized that and talked about that. I think about the number of times I had to push back on some Harvard economist who was saying that the economy was great, and actually everyone was wrong, and all these millions of Michiganders just didn\xe2\x80\x99t understand their own pocketbook. I mean, I called B.S. on that over and over and over again in the past 19 months.

How are you feeling about the midterms? Are Democrats run down and exhausted, or are they starting to perk up about opportunities in the future?

We\xe2\x80\x99re literally two weeks from the election. It\xe2\x80\x99s a little early. We haven\xe2\x80\x99t finished those postmortems; we haven\xe2\x80\x99t figured out how we\xe2\x80\x99re going to change. So I think it\xe2\x80\x99s just a little bit early to feel that enthusiasm. What is important, though, is people are not sitting around depressed. They\xe2\x80\x99re thinking about how to put some gas in the tank now, so that they are ready to push back when needed when Trump is sworn in.
